company.name: Dominion Energy
company.description: Dominion Energy is a regulated U.S. utility company providing electricity and natural gas service.

overview.title: Business profile
paragraphs: Dominion Energy is a U.S. regulated utility holding company centered on electric generation, transmission, distribution, and gas distribution infrastructure. Its core operating exposure is in Virginia, South Carolina, North Carolina, and nearby regulated service territories.

The company matters in the Free The World registry because its moat is mostly physical infrastructure, regulated franchises, grid interconnection, customer billing relationships, and capital-intensive reliability obligations rather than software lock-in.

overview.title: Registry lens
paragraphs: Dominion's products are not conventional discretionary products; they are essential utility services delivered through monopoly or quasi-monopoly networks. That makes direct replacement difficult, but it also makes the decentralization question unusually concrete: distributed generation, open energy management, demand response, microgrids, and electrification can move some control from the utility edge toward households, campuses, and communities.

A credible disruption path is unlikely to delete the regulated utility. It is more likely to compress parts of the load-growth, generation-planning, billing, and customer-control stack by making smaller participants more capable of producing, storing, shifting, and coordinating energy locally.

moatNarrative: Dominion's moat is high because regulated electric and gas utilities operate with exclusive or near-exclusive service territories, large sunk network assets, regulatory rate-setting processes, and public-service reliability duties. Replicating generation fleets, transmission links, substations, distribution lines, gas mains, permitting relationships, and dispatch capabilities is not realistic for ordinary entrants.

The moat is not primarily brand-driven. It comes from a franchise-like infrastructure position where customers usually cannot choose a parallel utility for the same meter, and where capital recovery is mediated through state and federal regulators.
decentralizationNarrative: Dominion is structurally hard to decentralize at the whole-company level because electricity and gas delivery require physical networks, safety obligations, balancing, and regulated cost recovery. However, parts of the service stack are increasingly decentralizable: rooftop solar, batteries, controllable loads, community microgrids, open demand-response protocols, and energy-management systems can shift decisions closer to customers.

Natural gas service is less decentralizable than electricity because it depends on fuel extraction, pipelines, distribution safety, and appliance infrastructure. The more credible disruption route is substitution: electrified heating, district thermal loops, efficiency, and local renewable generation reduce gas throughput rather than creating a peer-to-peer gas network.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 176-200.

moat.rationale: Dominion operates regulated electric and gas utility infrastructure with large physical assets, protected service territories, regulatory oversight, and reliability obligations that create unusually high barriers to direct entry.

decentralizability.rationale: The utility franchise itself is difficult to decentralize, but customer-side generation, storage, demand response, microgrids, and open energy controls can decentralize meaningful portions of generation and load coordination.

profitability.rationale: Dominion reports regulated utility earnings supported by rate-base recovery and essential-service demand, although profits remain sensitive to fuel, interest rates, capital plans, and regulatory outcomes.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate trailing valuation based on public market quote and reported earnings context; utility P/E should be treated as market-sensitive and refreshed before investment use.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap listed Dominion Energy around $58.87 billion in market capitalization near review time.

products.name: Electric utility service
products.summary: Dominion provides regulated electricity generation, transmission, distribution, reliability, customer service, and billing in its service territories.
products.whyItMatters: Electric service is the core infrastructure layer that determines how households, businesses, data centers, and local governments access power, connect distributed resources, and participate in demand flexibility.
replacementSketch: The near-term replacement pattern is not a parallel private utility. It is a layered local-energy stack: rooftop or community solar, batteries, controllable loads, open energy-management software, and microgrid coordination that reduce the amount of centralized generation and dispatch a customer needs from the incumbent.
Over time, more customers could treat the utility grid as a resilience and settlement backbone while more production, storage, and load-shaping happens locally.

alternatives.name: OpenEMS
alternatives.summary: OpenEMS is an open-source energy-management platform for integrating batteries, photovoltaic systems, EV charging, meters, and other controllable energy assets.

openness.rationale: OpenEMS publishes source code and documentation for a modular energy-management system, making it meaningfully inspectable and extensible.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The platform is aimed at controlling distributed assets such as batteries, PV, EV charging, and local energy devices rather than centralizing all decisions in a utility billing system.

readiness.rationale: OpenEMS is a real project with documentation and deployments, but integrating it into regulated utility contexts still requires qualified engineering, hardware compatibility, and local interconnection compliance.

costLeverage.rationale: Open software can reduce vendor lock-in in energy controls, but the dominant costs remain hardware, installation, permitting, grid interconnection, and maintenance.

disruptionConcepts.name: Community microgrid coordination
disruptionConcepts.summary: Neighborhoods, campuses, and commercial clusters use distributed generation, batteries, controllable loads, and open coordination software to form microgrids that can reduce peak demand, improve resilience, and localize part of the value now bundled into utility generation and distribution planning.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: If more load can be produced, stored, and dispatched locally, the incumbent utility remains important but loses some control over marginal capacity, resilience planning, and customer energy decisions.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is local operational control rather than Bitcoin. Open energy controls and interoperable hardware let multiple site owners coordinate assets without depending on a single proprietary utility platform.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Participants enroll generation, batteries, meters, and controllable loads into a local controller or federation of controllers; dispatch rules prioritize resilience, cost savings, and grid-service commitments.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Meters, inverter telemetry, signed device events, and utility-grade settlement data verify production and curtailment. Cheating is constrained by physical metering, audit logs, interconnection rules, and penalties for false performance claims, although device spoofing remains a real design risk.
failureModes: Interconnection rules, tariffs, and utility approval processes can slow deployment or limit export economics.
Local controllers can fail, mis-dispatch assets, or become proprietary chokepoints if open standards are not maintained.
Cybersecurity and meter-spoofing risks rise as more devices participate in dispatch.
adoptionPath: Start with campuses, municipalities, hospitals, and commercial parks that already have clear resilience needs.
Add customer-sited batteries, solar, backup generation, and controllable loads under open energy-management software.
Aggregate verified flexibility into utility demand-response or capacity programs where regulation allows.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Microgrids directly move generation, storage, and dispatch decisions toward local asset owners while still using the wider grid as backup and settlement infrastructure.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Demand-response standards and energy-management platforms provide a credible coordination basis, but settlement and dispatch still depend on utility tariffs and local regulation.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The hardware and software primitives exist, but projects are site-specific and require engineering, permitting, financing, cybersecurity, and interconnection approvals.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept pressures utility peak-load planning and resilience services more than it replaces the regulated grid outright.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open demand flexibility market
disruptionConcepts.summary: Homes and businesses expose verifiable load flexibility from thermostats, EV chargers, water heaters, batteries, and industrial loads into open demand-response markets instead of leaving dispatch primarily inside utility programs or proprietary aggregators.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure changes when customers can sell verified flexibility as a programmable resource, reducing the utility's need to build or procure peak generation and giving end users a clearer economic role.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Bitcoin is not required. The decentralization mechanism is an open marketplace and verifiable device telemetry that lets many small participants coordinate demand flexibility without a single proprietary aggregator controlling all access.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Devices or site controllers publish availability, price, and constraints; buyers such as utilities, grid operators, or aggregators purchase curtailment or load shifting; settlement is based on measured baseline performance.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: The system compares signed meter and device data against baselines, with penalties or reputation loss for non-performance. The hard problem is preventing baseline gaming, collusion among aggregators, and spoofed device telemetry.
failureModes: Baseline manipulation can overstate demand reductions.
Retail tariffs and market rules may block small customers from participating directly.
Customer fatigue can reduce available flexibility during extreme weather or high-price events.
adoptionPath: Begin with large commercial, industrial, and municipal loads where interval metering already exists.
Expand to residential batteries, EV chargers, smart thermostats, and water heaters through open standards.
Use audited telemetry and transparent settlement rules to let smaller aggregators compete with incumbent utility programs.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Demand flexibility markets distribute grid-balancing participation across many customer-owned devices and sites.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: OpenADR and virtual power plant models show a credible coordination pattern, though market access and settlement remain jurisdiction-specific.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The device and communications primitives are available, but customer recruitment, metering, baselines, cybersecurity, and tariff design are non-trivial.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Verified flexibility can defer some peak infrastructure and generation needs, but the incumbent utility still operates the network and often controls program design.

products.name: Natural gas service
products.summary: Dominion provides regulated natural gas distribution service, including pipeline distribution, customer connections, metering, safety operations, and billing in gas service areas.
products.whyItMatters: Gas service shapes home heating, water heating, industrial heat, and utility infrastructure investment; it is also a major target for substitution by electrification, efficiency, and local thermal systems.
replacementSketch: The most credible replacement path is not decentralized gas production for ordinary households. It is demand reduction and substitution: heat pumps, building electrification, thermal storage, weatherization, and district or campus energy systems that reduce dependence on delivered fossil gas.
Open controls can matter at the edge by coordinating electric loads and thermal storage so customers can leave gas appliances without simply shifting all peak burden back to the utility.

disruptionConcepts.name: Community thermal electrification
disruptionConcepts.summary: Buildings replace gas heating and water heating with heat pumps, weatherization, thermal storage, and coordinated electric controls so communities reduce gas throughput while managing the new electric load more intelligently.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: Gas distribution demand weakens when thermal loads become controllable electric loads that can be paired with local solar, storage, and flexible demand programs.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is customer and community control over thermal energy decisions, not Bitcoin. Open controls and interoperable devices let households and buildings coordinate heating demand without being locked into a single utility program.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Households, landlords, municipalities, and installers coordinate retrofits; local controllers schedule heat pumps, water heaters, and batteries around comfort, price, weather, and grid signals.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Utility bills, device telemetry, building audits, and measured gas-meter reductions verify performance. False claims are constrained by before-and-after consumption records, although weather normalization and rebound effects can complicate attribution.
failureModes: Poorly designed electrification can raise winter peak demand and shift stress to the electric grid.
Upfront retrofit cost, landlord-tenant split incentives, and contractor shortages can slow adoption.
Cold-weather performance, building envelope quality, and backup heat requirements vary by site.
adoptionPath: Start with municipal buildings, schools, campuses, and homes replacing end-of-life gas equipment.
Pair heat pumps with weatherization, thermal storage, and open demand-response controls.
Aggregate verified electric flexibility so gas displacement does not simply create unmanaged electric peaks.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept reduces dependence on centralized gas delivery and shifts some thermal decisions to customer-owned devices and local coordination.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Heat pumps and virtual power plant coordination are credible, but community-scale rollout requires financing, contractors, incentives, and tariff alignment.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Heat-pump and building-efficiency technology is commercially available, though implementation depends heavily on building stock and local labor capacity.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Large-scale electrification can reduce gas throughput and future gas infrastructure needs, but it is gradual and often still increases reliance on the electric utility side of the same company.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new Dominion Energy bundle with two products: regulated electric utility service and natural gas service. The refresh emphasizes Dominion's high regulated-infrastructure moat, moderate decentralization pressure from distributed energy, and credible disruption concepts around community microgrids, open demand flexibility, and thermal electrification.
